lenses.guidance: Two events frame the chapter — water from rock (vv 1-7) and Amalek (vv 8-16) — both naming places: Massah/Meribah (v 7) and Yahweh-Nissi (v 15). The narrative structure binds wilderness provision and battle as twin tests.

Moses strikes the rock (v 6); decades later he strikes a second rock and is barred from the land for it (Num 20:11-12). The pattern teaches: the rock is struck once. Christ struck once for sins (Heb 9:28) is the antitype.

'The rock that followed them was Christ' (1 Cor 10:4) — Paul's grammar makes the rock at Massah (v 6) not similar to Christ but identified as him. Wilderness sustenance becomes Christology, not metaphor.

Amalek's defeat (v 13) begins a thread — Deut 25:17-19 commands remembrance, 1 Sam 15 sees Saul disobey it, Esther 3 names Haman as 'the Agagite'. The canonical line runs from this battle to the Persian palace.

Moses' arms grow heavy; Aaron and Hur hold them up 'one on each side' (v 12). Intercession is communal — when prayer flags, others pray with you. Today, ask someone to hold your arms when you cannot lift them yourself.
